answers.value: Post-op left ACL reconstruction: avoid pivoting/twisting on surgical leg for first 12 weeks, no running or jumping until cleared by surgeon, avoid open-chain knee extension 0–30° with resistance for first 8 weeks, monitor for increased swelling, redness, or warmth in the knee or calf; stop session if sharp joint pain, locking, or giving way reported.

answers.value: Include progressive return-to-running program beginning at approximately 12 weeks post-op if adequate strength and neuromuscular control demonstrated; integrate soccer-specific drills (cutting, lateral shuffles, controlled deceleration) in later phases per protocol and patient tolerance.

answers.value:
Patient is a 32-year-old recreational soccer player and software engineer who works primarily at a computer (approximately 8–9 hours per day), currently on a hybrid schedule (3 days in office, 2 days remote). He reports increased stiffness and swelling at the end of the workday, especially after prolonged sitting or back-to-back meetings where he does not stand up for over an hour.

Please emphasize: 
- Education on workstation setup and strategies to stand or walk briefly every 45–60 minutes (e.g., setting phone/computer reminders, taking calls standing, walking to refill water bottle, using stairs instead of elevator when appropriate).
- Detailed home exercise program that can be realistically broken into short segments (10–15 minutes) before work, during lunch, and in the evening so he can adhere despite his schedule.
- Clear written and verbal guidance on what level of post-exercise soreness and swelling is acceptable versus concerning, as patient tends to "push through" discomfort due to his athletic background and has expressed anxiety about potentially re-injuring the graft.

Medical history relevant to therapy:
- No history of diabetes, heart disease, or chronic lung disease.
- Mild, well-controlled exercise-induced asthma (uses albuterol inhaler as needed; rarely requires it, mostly with high-intensity cardio in cold weather). No recent exacerbations.
- No history of DVT or clotting disorders.
- No prior surgeries on the left knee; had a right ankle sprain 5 years ago fully resolved with prior PT.

Medications:
- Ibuprofen 400–600 mg by mouth as needed for pain, typically no more than once daily, 3–4 days per week at most.
- Occasional acetaminophen 500 mg as needed for breakthrough pain (instructed not to take concurrently with ibuprofen without spacing doses and staying within recommended daily limits).

Allergies:
- No known drug allergies.
- Seasonal/environmental allergies (pollen, dust) only.

Goals (patient-stated):
- Short term (next 2–3 months): Walk without limp or pain, climb stairs normally, sit through a 2-hour meeting without significant stiffness, and be able to perform basic gym exercises (bike, light leg press, bodyweight squats) safely.
- Long term (6–9 months): Return to recreational soccer (1–2 games per week) and light hiking with his wife without fear of the knee giving out. Patient is highly motivated but needs structured progression and clear objective criteria (strength tests, hop tests, etc.) to know he is on track.

Communication preferences:
- Primary language: English.
- Prefers detailed explanations of what each exercise is targeting and how it relates to graft protection and long-term stability.

Please fax progress notes to the surgeon at routine post-op milestones (approximately 12 weeks and 6 months) or sooner if concerns arise regarding range of motion plateau, recurrent effusion, or instability.
